Tesla Faces Contradictory Signals: Energy Booms While Auto Margins Compress Further

By Robert Hayes | March 8, 2025 | Barron's

Tesla presents one of the most complex investment cases in the market today, with its two main business lines sending sharply divergent signals.

BEARISH — AUTOMOTIVE:
Tesla's core automotive business continues to deteriorate. Q1 2025 deliveries of 386,810 fell 13% year-over-year, and the average transaction price declined to approximately $43,200, down from $46,100 a year ago. Automotive gross margin has compressed to 16.3% from 19.4%, as Tesla engages in an ongoing price war with BYD, which now outsells Tesla globally by nearly 2:1.

The Cybertruck has failed to become the volume driver Tesla hoped, with estimated Q1 deliveries of only 15,000 units. Manufacturing costs remain above selling price, making each Cybertruck sold a money-losing proposition. The Semi remains in very limited production.

BULLISH — ENERGY:
Tesla's energy storage business is firing on all cylinders. Megapack deployments hit 10.4 GWh in Q1, a quarterly record, and the segment's gross margin expanded to 24.6%. The energy order backlog now exceeds $12 billion. Analysts at Piper Sandler estimate Tesla Energy could generate $15 billion in revenue by 2026 with 25%+ margins.

WILDCARD — FSD AND ROBOTAXI:
The California CPUC approval for limited robotaxi operations is a step forward, but the requirement for safety operators significantly limits the economic proposition. Waymo already operates fully driverless in the same markets.

"Tesla is essentially two companies in one," wrote Dan Ives at Wedbush. "Investors need to decide if the energy and autonomy upside is worth weathering the automotive margin storm."

Tesla shares are down 18% year-to-date as investors weigh these conflicting narratives.
